title: SPI Overview
description: What SPI is, how the 4-wire bus works, and when to choose SPI over UART or I2C.
body: SPI vs UART: The Key Difference UART is asynchronous — it reconstructs timing from the data itself. SPI takes a different approach: a dedicated clock line tells the receiver exactly when to sample each bit. This makes SPI faster and more reliable at high speeds. The Four Wires SPI uses four signals: | Signal | Full Name | Direction | |--------|-----------|-----------| | SCLK | Serial Clock | Master → Slave | | MOSI | Master Out Slave In | Master → Slave | | MISO | Master In Slave Out | Slave → Master | | CS / SS | Chip Select / Slave Select | Master → Slave | The master always controls the clock. Data is exchanged simultaneously on MOSI and MISO — SPI is inherently full-duplex. Clock Polarity and Phase (CPOL/CPHA) SPI has four modes based on two settings: - CPOL (Clock Polarity) — idle clock state: 0 = low, 1 = high - CPHA (Clock Phase) — when to sample: 0 = leading edge, 1 = trailing edge | Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Sample On | |------|------|------|-----------| | 0 | 0 | 0 | Rising edge | | 1 | 0 | 1 | Falling edge | | 2 | 1 | 0 | Falling edge | | 3 | 1 | 1 | Rising edge | Mode 0 is the most common. Always check your device datasheet — mismatched mode is the 1 SPI debugging problem. Initiating a Transfer The master pulls CS low to select a slave, clocks out data on MOSI while simultaneously reading MISO, then releases CS high: Multiple Slaves Unlike I2C, SPI has no built-in addressing scheme. Each slave needs its own CS line: The master can only talk to one slave at a time (exactly one CS line low at once). When to Choose SPI Use SPI when you need: - High throughput (10+ Mbps easily achievable) - Simple, deterministic timing - Communication with ADCs, DACs, displays, flash memory, SD cards Don't use SPI when: - You have many devices (too many CS pins) - You need to add devices without rewiring (use I2C) - Cable length is significant (SPI has no drive strength standard)

title: What is UART?
description: An introduction to UART serial communication — what it is, why it exists, and where you'll find it in embedded systems.
body: The Problem UART Solves Microcontrollers need to talk to the outside world. They need to send debug messages to your laptop, receive commands from a host, or exchange data with another chip. But how? The simplest possible answer is two wires: one for sending (TX), one for receiving (RX). That's the heart of UART. What UART Actually Is UART stands for Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter. It's not a protocol owned by a standards body — it's a hardware specification for a serial communication method that has been around since the 1960s. Key characteristics: - Asynchronous — no shared clock signal between devices - Full-duplex — can send and receive simultaneously - Point-to-point — connects exactly two devices The Hardware Interface A UART connection uses two signal lines: | Pin | Direction | Description | |-----|-----------|-------------| | TX | Output | Transmit data | | RX | Input | Receive data | | GND | — | Common ground (always needed) | > Critical: TX on one device connects to RX on the other, and vice versa. Connecting TX→TX is a common beginner mistake. How Data Moves UART sends data one bit at a time, framed within a UART frame: - Idle state — the line sits HIGH when nothing is being sent - Start bit — always 0, signals the receiver to start sampling - Data bits — typically 8 bits, LSB first - Stop bit — always 1, marks end of frame Baud Rate: The Timing Agreement Since UART is asynchronous, both sides must agree on one thing: how fast to send bits. This is the baud rate — bits per second. If the baud rates don't match, you'll receive garbage. This is the 1 cause of UART debugging headaches. Real Hardware Example On an STM32 microcontroller, UART is called USART (the extra S stands for Synchronous — it can optionally use a clock). Here's the simplest possible transmit: When to Use UART UART is the right choice when you need: - A simple debug console (connect to a USB-serial adapter) - Communication with GPS modules, GSM modems, Bluetooth modules - Talking to another microcontroller over a short cable It's not the right choice for: - Multiple devices on the same bus (use SPI or I2C instead) - Very high speeds over long distances - Synchronous data that needs a clock reference

title: Baud Rate and Framing
description: How UART timing works, how to calculate bit periods, and what the common frame configurations mean.
body: Bit Period: The Foundation of UART Timing The baud rate tells both devices how long each bit lasts. The math is simple: At 115200 baud: Every bit holds its value for exactly that long. The receiver samples the line at the middle of each bit period — this gives the most margin for timing drift. How the Receiver Locks On Without a shared clock, the receiver must determine when each bit starts. It does this by detecting the falling edge of the start bit: After detecting the start bit, the receiver waits 1.5 bit periods to sample D0, then exactly 1 bit period for each subsequent data bit. Frame Format: 8N1 The most common UART frame format is 8N1: - 8 — 8 data bits - N — No parity bit - 1 — 1 stop bit This is almost universally the default. When someone says "UART at 115200", they mean 115200 8N1. Parity: Optional Error Detection UART supports an optional parity bit inserted between the last data bit and the stop bit: | Parity | Meaning | |--------|---------| | None (N) | No parity bit — most common | | Even (E) | Bit set so total 1s in frame is even | | Odd (O) | Bit set so total 1s in frame is odd | Parity catches single-bit errors but not burst errors. For reliable comms, use a proper CRC over the entire packet instead. Stop Bits: 1 or 2? Most systems use 1 stop bit. Two stop bits exist for legacy compatibility with older hardware that needed more time to process between frames. You'll essentially never need 2 stop bits on modern microcontrollers. Clock Accuracy Requirements Because UART is asynchronous, the transmitter and receiver clocks must stay synchronized across a full frame (10 bits for 8N1). The cumulative drift must not exceed ±0.5 bit periods. In practice, the clocks must agree to within about ±2–3%. Most crystal oscillators are accurate to ±50 ppm (0.005%), so timing errors are rarely a problem unless you're using an RC oscillator. Calculating Max Baud Rate The maximum baud rate is limited by: 1. GPIO slew rate — the pin's max switching speed 2. Peripheral clock — the UART peripheral needs at least 16× oversampling 3. Cable capacitance — longer cables limit speed On an STM32F4 running at 84 MHz: Practically, 1–3 Mbaud is reliable on short PCB traces. Common Configuration Table | Application | Baud Rate | Format | Notes | |-------------|-----------|--------|-------| | Arduino serial monitor | 9600 | 8N1 | Default, very tolerant | | GPS modules (NMEA) | 9600 | 8N1 | Industry standard | | USB-serial debug | 115200 | 8N1 | Most adapters default | | HC-05 Bluetooth | 38400 | 8N1 | Module default | | High-speed MCU comms | 1000000 | 8N1 | Requires short cables |
